metrics_to_collect.hint: Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: (DE_share_t / DE_share_t-3)^(1/3) - 1. Requires at least 4 annual data points. Undefined when base-year DE is negative or zero — record as N/A. M&A activity can distort growth rates if share count changed materially.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: (FEAUM_t / FEAUM_t-3)^(1/3) - 1. Smooths year-to-year volatility; provides a medium-term growth trajectory. Requires 4 data points. Includes inorganic growth; firms with recent IPOs or major restructuring may have non-comparable base-year figures.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Total capital raised in period / beginning-of-period AUM * 100. Includes new fund closes, continuation vehicles, and incremental commitments to existing open-end vehicles; excludes reinvested distributions. Fundraising is lumpy — large flagship fund closes create spikes.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Sum(share_i^2) * 10,000, where share_i is the fraction of total AUM in asset class i. Higher HHI = more concentrated (10,000 = single asset class). Lower HHI signals more diversified fee streams. Standardize across firms using 5-7 asset class categories: private equity, credit, real assets/infrastructure, hedge fund solutions, insurance solutions, other.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Perpetual/open-end/evergreen AUM / total AUM * 100. Permanent capital includes publicly listed vehicles (BDCs, REITs), perpetual private funds, insurance general accounts, and long-duration vehicles with no fixed end date. Higher permanent capital implies more predictable management fee streams with lower re-fundraising risk.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Credit AUM / total AUM * 100. Credit strategies include direct lending, distressed debt, structured credit (CLOs), mezzanine, and investment-grade private credit. Excludes equity-oriented special situations. Credit is the fastest-growing segment in alternatives — high exposure may signal growth optionality.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: FRE / management fee revenue * 100. Measures operating leverage on the fee-earning business, excluding performance income. FRE is non-GAAP with significant definition differences: (1) whether equity-based compensation is deducted, (2) whether transaction/advisory fees are included in revenue, (3) treatment of distribution/placement costs. Document each firm's definition.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Total compensation and benefits expense / total revenue * 100. Lower ratios indicate greater operating leverage. Revenue denominator is volatile due to performance fees — computing on fee revenue only may yield a cleaner signal. Partnership structures may obscure true compensation costs.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Management fee revenue / average FEAUM * 10,000 (expressed as FRE/FEAUM in basis points). Use average FEAUM (beginning + end of period / 2) to avoid timing distortions. Higher rates indicate pricing power and/or mix tilted toward higher-fee strategies. Blended rate masks strategy-level variation.

metrics_to_collect.metric_name: Performance Fee Share of Total Revenue
metrics_to_collect.hint: Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: (Performance/incentive fees + realized carried interest) / total revenue * 100. Measures revenue cyclicality and upside optionality. Highly volatile year-to-year depending on vintage harvesting cycles. Market may discount high performance fee share (preferring stable fees) or reward it (as optionality) — direction of correlation is theoretically ambiguous.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Total compensation and benefits expense / average FEAUM * 10,000 (in basis points). Measures the human capital cost per unit of fee-earning assets. Lower values indicate that the firm extracts more fee revenue per compensation dollar spent at scale. Use total comp & benefits from income statement (including equity-based comp if disclosed separately). For firms that do not separate comp into fee-related vs. performance-related, use total comp. Subject to 60% coverage rule.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: General and administrative expense / average FEAUM * 10,000 (in basis points). Captures non-compensation overhead costs (occupancy, technology, professional services, travel) normalized by scale. Lower values indicate leaner infrastructure. G&A line items are inconsistently labeled across firms — use the broadest non-compensation operating expense line available. Exclude fund-level expenses passed through to LPs. Subject to 60% coverage rule.

metrics_to_collect.metric_name: OpEx Growth minus Fee Revenue Growth
metrics_to_collect.hint: Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Trailing 1-year total operating expense growth rate minus trailing 1-year fee revenue growth rate (in percentage points). Negative values indicate positive operating leverage (costs growing slower than revenue). Positive values indicate cost creep or diseconomies of scale. Use total segment or company-level operating expenses and management fee revenue. One-time items (restructuring, litigation) should be flagged but not excluded unless clearly non-recurring. Subject to 60% coverage rule.

Metric may be derivable — search for component inputs: Total headcount / FEAUM (in USD bn). Lower values indicate greater scale efficiency — fewer people needed per unit of fee-earning assets. Subject to 60% coverage rule — reclassify as contextual-only if fewer than 60% of universe firms disclose headcount.
